title: Getting Started with Docker
content:
### Installing Docker Desktop

Docker Desktop is the easiest way to get Docker running on your machine. It includes Docker Engine, Docker CLI, Docker Compose, and a GUI interface.

Download Docker Desktop from [docker.com/products/docker-desktop](https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop) and install it for your operating system (Windows, macOS, or Linux).

### Verifying the Installation

After installation, open a terminal and verify Docker is working:

```bash
docker --version
```

This prints the installed Docker version. You can also get more detailed system information:

```bash
docker info
```

### Hello World

The classic first step is running the hello-world container:

```bash
docker run hello-world
```

This command pulls the `hello-world` image from Docker Hub (if not cached locally) and runs it in a container that prints a welcome message and exits.

### Docker Hub Account

Docker Hub is the default public registry for container images. Create a free account at [hub.docker.com](https://hub.docker.com). You'll need it to:
- Pull public images (like `nginx`, `node`, `python`)
- Push your own images
- Access private repositories

### Docker Engine vs Docker Desktop

- **Docker Engine**: The core server (daemon) and CLI — runs on Linux, typically installed via package managers
- **Docker Desktop**: Includes Docker Engine plus a VM (on Windows/macOS), GUI dashboard, Kubernetes integration, and extra developer tools

On Windows and macOS, Docker Desktop runs the Docker Engine inside a lightweight VM since those OSes don't natively support Linux containers.
codeExample:
docker --version
docker run hello-world
docker info

quiz.question: Which command checks the installed Docker version?
Why does Docker Desktop use a VM on Windows and macOS?
options: Because those OSes don't natively support Linux containers
